content.problemStatement:
Chef has a permutation PPP of length 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N. He can perform the following operation on PPP:


Select an index iii (1≤i<2⋅N)(1 \le i \lt 2 \cdot N)(1≤i<2⋅N) and swap PiP_iPi​ and Pi+1P_{i + 1}Pi+1​.
Chef would call the permutation PPP good, if the **maximum** element of the first half of PPP is **less** than the **minimum** element of the second half of PPP.

Formally max⁡(1≤i≤N)Pi<min⁡(N<i≤2⋅N)Pi\displaystyle \max_{(1 \le i \le N)} P_i \lt \min_{(N \lt i \le 2 \cdot N)} P_i(1≤i≤N)max​Pi​<(N<i≤2⋅N)min​Pi​.

Find the **minimum** number of operations Chef needs to apply to make PPP good.

**Note:** A permutation of length NNN is an array where every integer from 111 to NNN occurs exactly once.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — half the size of the permutation PPP.
The second line of each test case contains 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N space-separated integers P1,P2,…,P2⋅NP_1, P_2, \dots, P_{2 \cdot N}P1​,P2​,…,P2⋅N​ denoting the permutation PPP.


Output Format
For each test case, output the minimum number of operations required to make PPP good.
constraints: P is a permutation of length 2·N
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (half the length of the permutation). The second line contains 2·N space‑separated integers P1,P2,…,P2N – a permutation of numbers 1…2N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the minimum number of adjacent‑swap operations required to make the permutation good.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Inversions are (3,1) and (3,2) → 2 swaps.\nTest case 2: Already good, no inversions.\nTest case 3: Every large element precedes every small element, N=3 gives 3·3=9 inversions.
The only large element (2) is before the small element (1), so one adjacent swap is needed.
First half already contains numbers 1..4, second half 5..8, so no swaps are required.

solution.approach: A permutation is good iff the first half contains exactly the N smallest numbers (1…N) and the second half contains the N largest numbers (N+1…2N). Therefore we only need to move every element ≤ N to the left side. Each adjacent swap can eliminate one inversion where a large element (value > N) appears before a small element (value ≤ N). The minimum number of swaps equals the total number of such inversions. Scan the array from left to right, keep a counter of how many large elements have been seen so far, and whenever a small element is encountered add this counter to the answer. Use 64‑bit integer for the sum.
solution.timeComplexity: O(2·N) per test case, i.e., O(N). Over all test cases O(∑N).

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        total = 2 * N
        large = 0
        ans = 0
        for _ in range(total):
            x = next(it)
            if x > N:
                large += 1
            else:
                ans += large
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
